one_line_takeaway: AutoMIA shows that an LLM-agent evolutionary loop reasoning over natural-language attack designs (rather than raw code) can discover novel membership-inference signal functions that beat both hand-crafted SOTA and a general code-evolution baseline (OpenEvolve) by up to 0.18 absolute AUC across black-box LLM and gray-box VLM settings.
key_finding: AutoMIA outperforms both the human-designed baseline and OpenEvolve across nearly all tested settings. For black-box LLMs (Table 1), AutoMIA achieves AUC 0.687 (Pythia-1.4B/ArXiv), 0.750 (Pythia-1.4B/Github), 0.726 (Pythia-1.4B/Pubmed), 0.703 (OPT-7B/ArXiv), 0.653 (OPT-7B/Github), 0.707 (OPT-7B/Pubmed) versus human-designed baselines of 0.547, 0.664, 0.689, 0.542, 0.620, 0.676 respectively, and versus OpenEvolve's 0.593, 0.719, 0.697, 0.597, 0.609, 0.660. The largest single-setting gain cited in the text: OPT-7B on ArXiv, AUC rises from 0.54 (baseline) to 0.7 (AutoMIA) and TPR@1%FPR rises from 0.02 to 0.1 -- the paper's headline 'up to 0.18 absolute AUC improvement' figure. For gray-box VLMs (Table 2), AutoMIA raises image-logit AUC from a baseline of 0.594 (DALL-E) / 0.590 (Flickr) to 0.752 / 0.770, and text-logit AUC from 0.605/0.736 to 0.705/0.719. The ablation (Table 3, OPT-7B/ArXiv) shows full AutoMIA at AUC 0.703; removing the natural-language idea/thoughts representation drops AUC to 0.594; removing the Exploiter drops it to 0.637; removing the Explorer drops it to 0.674 -- indicating the Exploiter (refinement) contributes more than the Explorer (novelty search) in this setting, and that natural-language design representation is important versus AlphaEvolve-style raw code evolution. Discovered attack types are qualitatively described: geometric edit-distance and rare-trigram/rarity-weighted signals for text (best on ArXiv/Pubmed used edit-distance; best on Github used rare n-gram signals), and a rank-stability signal (adds noise to logits, checks token-rank stability) for VLM logits. Transferability analysis found the ArXiv-discovered MIA transfers well to Mathematics, Pubmed, and HackerNews but not to Github. Evolving from scratch without a human-designed seed reaches performance within <1% AUC of the seeded run. Providing dataset/domain context to the agents yields higher-quality MIAs at most iterations versus withholding it. Claude Haiku-4.5 as backbone found a stronger MIA (AUC 0.72) than Qwen-3-80B (AUC 0.69) on Pythia-1.4B/Pubmed, versus a 0.55 AUC human baseline.
core_contribution: The paper introduces AutoMIA, an agentic system that uses LLM agents to automate the design and implementation of the MIA signal-computation step (Stage 2 of the standard membership-inference-attack pipeline). AutoMIA runs an evolutionary loop with two design agents (Explorer, which generates novel candidate signal designs via a novelty-guided refinement loop checked by a Novelty Judge LLM; Exploiter, which refines top-performing existing designs sampled proportional to |AUC-0.5|) and three execution agents (Programmer, which translates a natural-language design into Python code; Executor, which runs and validates it; Analyzer, which reads results and produces analysis). All attempts (idea/design/code/results/analysis) are stored in a shared database supporting both dense (embedding) and sparse (BM25) retrieval so later agents can learn from prior successes and failures. A key design choice, explicitly contrasted with AlphaEvolve/OpenEvolve, is that AutoMIA evolves attack ideas and designs in natural language rather than evolving raw code directly.
threat_model: The paper targets two settings: (1) black-box MIA against LLMs, where the attacker/auditor can only access the final generated text output of the target LLM (no logits or auxiliary information), evaluated on the MIMIR benchmark; and (2) gray-box MIA against Vision-Language Models, where the attacker can access the target model's intermediate representations (logits), reusing the setting of Li et al. 2024. In both settings the goal is membership inference (determine whether a specific data point x was part of a private training set D_train), formalized as learning a signal function f: O x X -> R from model output o and input x, optimized on a design/training split and evaluated on a held-out test split for generalization. The paper explicitly frames this as a red-teaming/auditing tool (privacy leakage estimation, worst-case training-data retention), while also stating a dual-use concern that the same system could help adversaries find stronger attacks against deployed models.
limitations: The authors state these explicitly: (1) Design scope -- AutoMIA automates only the signal-computation stage of the MIA pipeline, not the query/inference strategy (Stage 1), which they note would be far more computationally expensive to automate for large models. (2) Execution infrastructure -- the code implementation/execution flow is simple relative to leading coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini-CLI) and runs in a pre-installed environment with a fixed library set; the authors report that roughly 15-20% of proposed designs could not be successfully implemented and executed, capping overall performance. (3) Limited benchmark coverage -- only two MIA settings (black-box LLM, gray-box VLM) were evaluated; domain-specific settings (e.g., healthcare, finance) are left to future work, and the search budget was capped at 100 iterations / 2 hours per setting for computational efficiency, with the authors noting more iterations would likely yield better performance. (4) Dual-use concern -- explicitly acknowledged: the same system framed as an auditing/red-teaming tool could equally help adversaries discover stronger attacks against deployed models.

datasets_used: MIMIR benchmark (ArXiv, Github, Pubmed subsets) for black-box LLM MIA
DALL-E dataset for gray-box VLM MIA
Flickr dataset for gray-box VLM MIA
Transferability evaluation additionally uses Mathematics, HackerNews, Wikipedia, Pile CC subsets
quantitative_results: Black-box LLM, Pythia-1.4B/ArXiv: AUC 0.687 (AutoMIA) vs 0.593 (OpenEvolve) vs 0.547 (Hallinan et al. baseline)
Black-box LLM, Pythia-1.4B/Github: AUC 0.750 (AutoMIA) vs 0.719 (OpenEvolve) vs 0.664 (baseline)
Black-box LLM, Pythia-1.4B/Pubmed: AUC 0.726 (AutoMIA) vs 0.697 (OpenEvolve) vs 0.689 (baseline)
Black-box LLM, OPT-7B/ArXiv: AUC 0.703 (AutoMIA) vs 0.597 (OpenEvolve) vs 0.542 (baseline); TPR@1%FPR 0.02->0.1 cited in text as headline gain
Black-box LLM, OPT-7B/Github: AUC 0.653 (AutoMIA) vs 0.609 (OpenEvolve) vs 0.620 (baseline)
Black-box LLM, OPT-7B/Pubmed: AUC 0.707 (AutoMIA) vs 0.660 (OpenEvolve) vs 0.676 (baseline)
Gray-box VLM, DALL-E image logits: AUC 0.752 (AutoMIA) vs 0.612 (OpenEvolve) vs 0.594 (Li et al. baseline)
Gray-box VLM, DALL-E text logits: AUC 0.705 (AutoMIA) vs 0.575 (OpenEvolve) vs 0.605 (baseline)
Gray-box VLM, Flickr image logits: AUC 0.770 (AutoMIA) vs 0.725 (OpenEvolve) vs 0.590 (baseline)
Gray-box VLM, Flickr text logits: AUC 0.719 (AutoMIA) vs 0.636 (OpenEvolve) vs 0.736 (baseline; only case where baseline beats AutoMIA on AUC per table)
Ablation (OPT-7B/ArXiv): full AutoMIA AUC 0.703; w/o Idea & Thoughts (code+score only) AUC 0.594; w/o Exploiter AUC 0.637; w/o Explorer AUC 0.674
LLM backbone comparison (Pythia-1.4B/Pubmed): AutoMIA+Claude Haiku-4.5 AUC 0.72 vs AutoMIA+Qwen-3-80B AUC 0.69 vs human baseline AUC 0.55
Evolving from scratch (no seed) vs seeded AutoMIA: end-performance AUC gap < 1%
Execution robustness: ~15-20% of proposed designs failed to implement/execute successfully
baselines_compared: Human-designed MIA (Hallinan et al. 2025, n-gram/max-coverage signal) for black-box LLM setting
Human-designed MIA (Li et al. 2024, Renyi entropy signal) for gray-box VLM setting
OpenEvolve (Sharma 2025), a community reimplementation of AlphaEvolve, used as a general-purpose code-evolution baseline with the same LLM backbone
